female-28-new-diagnosis.chiefComplaint: I've had this rash on my face and my hands hurt for weeks
female-28-new-diagnosis.variantDescription: New-onset SLE with malar rash, arthritis, and early nephritis

missedConsequences.Missed Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: Failure to diagnose Systemic Lupus Erythematosus in a timely manner can lead to significant morbidity.
missedConsequences.Delayed Treatment: Delayed recognition and treatment of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus worsens outcomes.

commonPitfalls: Anchoring on initial presentation without considering Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
Failing to obtain appropriate initial workup
Not recognizing red flags that warrant urgent evaluation
Premature closure before completing the differential diagnosis

mcqs.question: What is the most important initial diagnostic step for this patient?

options.text: Focused history and targeted laboratory studies
options.explanation: A focused history combined with targeted labs is the appropriate first step to narrow the differential.

options.text: Empiric treatment before any diagnostic workup
options.explanation: Empiric treatment without diagnosis risks treating the wrong condition.
This presentation warrants workup before reassurance.
Advanced imaging may be needed but basic workup should come first.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

sle-primary.name: Patient
sle-primary.chiefComplaint: Young woman with fatigue, joint pain, and facial rash
sle-primary.variantDescription: SLICC criteria, autoantibody profiles, lupus nephritis classification, flare management
keyFindings: Classic: young woman with malar rash, arthritis, serositis, renal/hematologic/neuro involvement
ANA screening (sensitive but not specific); anti-dsDNA (specific, correlates with nephritis activity)
Anti-Smith: most specific for SLE; Anti-Ro/La: neonatal lupus, photosensitivity
Lupus nephritis: Class III/IV (proliferative) most serious — need biopsy
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and ESR of 85 mm/hr is suspected of having giant cell arteritis. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Start high-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) immediately, then arrange temporal artery biopsy
options.explanation: Per ACR/EULAR guidelines, GCA with visual symptoms is an emergency. High-dose glucocorticoids must be started IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic even after several weeks of steroid therapy due to persistent inflammatory changes.

options.text: Schedule temporal artery biopsy first to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
options.explanation: Waiting for biopsy before treatment risks permanent vision loss. Biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without significantly affecting sensitivity.

options.text: Start low-dose prednisone (10 mg/day) and monitor symptoms for 1 week
options.explanation: Low-dose steroids are insufficient for GCA. The recommended dose is 40-60 mg/day for uncomplicated GCA, or 1 mg/kg/day (up to IV pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days) when visual symptoms are present.

options.text: Order CRP and IL-6 levels before making treatment decisions
options.explanation: While CRP and IL-6 are elevated in GCA, additional testing should not delay treatment in a patient with classic symptoms and elevated ESR. Vision loss can occur within hours.

rheumatoid-arthritis-primary.name: Patient
rheumatoid-arthritis-primary.chiefComplaint: Woman with bilateral hand swelling and prolonged morning stiffness
rheumatoid-arthritis-primary.variantDescription: Symmetric small joint involvement, RF vs anti-CCP, pannus formation, DMARDs, extra-articular manifestations
keyFindings: Symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis affecting MCP and PIP joints (spares DIP — that's OA)
Morning stiffness >1 hour (vs OA: <30 minutes, improves with rest)
Anti-CCP: most specific; RF: sensitive but less specific (present in many conditions)
Pannus formation: synovial proliferation → cartilage/bone erosion
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Rheumatoid Arthritis
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

gout-pseudogout-primary.name: Patient
gout-pseudogout-primary.chiefComplaint: Middle-aged man with acute severe pain in the big toe
gout-pseudogout-primary.variantDescription: Monosodium urate vs calcium pyrophosphate, crystal analysis, acute management, urate-lowering therapy
keyFindings: Gout: monosodium urate (MSU) crystals — needle-shaped, negatively birefringent (yellow parallel)
Pseudogout (CPPD): calcium pyrophosphate — rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent (blue parallel)
Gout: 1st MTP (podagra) classic; Pseudogout: knee most common
Acute gout treatment: NSAIDs, colchicine, or corticosteroids (NOT allopurinol during acute flare)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Gout & Pseudogout
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

options.text: Gout: monosodium urate (MSU) crystals — needle-shaped, negatively birefringent (yellow parallel)

spondyloarthropathy-primary.name: Patient
spondyloarthropathy-primary.chiefComplaint: 25-year-old man with chronic low back pain worse in the morning
spondyloarthropathy-primary.variantDescription: HLA-B27 association, ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, sacroiliitis
keyFindings: HLA-B27 associated: AS, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis
Ankylosing spondylitis: young male, inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness >30min, improves with exercise), sacroiliitis → bamboo spine
Reactive arthritis: 'can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree' (conjunctivitis, urethritis, arthritis) post-infection
Psoriatic arthritis: DIP involvement (distinguishes from RA), dactylitis, enthesitis, pencil-in-cup deformity
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Spondyloarthropathies
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

scleroderma-primary.name: Patient
scleroderma-primary.chiefComplaint: Woman with Raynaud phenomenon and progressive skin tightening
scleroderma-primary.variantDescription: Limited vs diffuse, CREST syndrome, anti-centromere vs anti-Scl-70, pulmonary complications
keyFindings: Limited (CREST): Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia
Limited: anti-centromere antibody; pulmonary arterial hypertension main cause of death
Diffuse: anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I); rapid skin thickening, renal crisis, pulmonary fibrosis
Scleroderma renal crisis: malignant HTN + microangiopathic hemolytic anemia → treat with ACE inhibitors
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

inflammatory-myopathy-primary.name: Patient
inflammatory-myopathy-primary.chiefComplaint: Woman with proximal muscle weakness and heliotrope rash
inflammatory-myopathy-primary.variantDescription: Dermatomyositis vs polymyositis, autoantibody profiles, malignancy association, CK elevation
keyFindings: Proximal muscle weakness (difficulty rising from chair, climbing stairs, overhead reaching)
Dermatomyositis: heliotrope rash (purple eyelids), Gottron papules (knuckles), shawl sign
Dermatomyositis: anti-Mi-2, anti-MDA5; associated with malignancy (ovarian, lung, GI) — screen at diagnosis
Polymyositis: anti-Jo-1 (antisynthetase syndrome: myositis + ILD + mechanic's hands + arthritis)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Inflammatory Myopathies
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

antiphospholipid-syndrome-primary.name: Patient
antiphospholipid-syndrome-primary.chiefComplaint: Young woman with recurrent DVTs and pregnancy losses
antiphospholipid-syndrome-primary.variantDescription: Thrombosis paradox (in vitro anticoagulant, in vivo procoagulant), pregnancy morbidity, lupus anticoagulant, diagnosis criteria
keyFindings: Arterial AND venous thrombosis + pregnancy morbidity
Antibodies: lupus anticoagulant (strongest predictor of thrombosis), anticardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein I
Paradox: 'anticoagulant' name because it prolongs PTT in vitro, but CAUSES thrombosis in vivo
Diagnosis: clinical criterion + laboratory criterion (positive on 2 occasions ≥12 weeks apart)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Antiphospholipid Syndrome
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

options.text: Arterial AND venous thrombosis + pregnancy morbidity
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.
